entries.title: Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier: Field Experimental Evidence of the Effects of AI on Knowledge Worker Productivity and Quality
entries.author: Dell'Acqua, Fabrizio and McFowland III, Edward and Mollick, Ethan and Lifshitz-Assaf, Hila and Kellogg, Katherine and Rajendran, Saran and Krayer, Lisa and Candelon, François and Lakhani, Karim
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Will AI take my job?
entries.hook: AI users outperformed non-users by 40% on some tasks — and did worse on others. Nobody could see the line. This is the study that made 'override' a survival skill.
entries.why_it_matters: The 'jagged frontier' is the empirical proof behind the Verification and Override instruments — AI fails confidently, and the failure zone is invisible.
entries.summary: A field experiment with 758 BCG consultants showed GPT-4 improved performance on tasks inside its capability frontier by 40% but decreased accuracy on tasks outside it by 19%. The study coined the concept of a 'jagged technological frontier' that workers cannot reliably perceive.

entries.title: How Should I Be Using A.I. Right Now?
entries.author: Klein, Ezra and Mollick, Ethan
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: The Wharton professor who uses AI more than anyone in academia sits down with the journalist who asks the best questions. The result is the most useful hour on AI you'll hear.
entries.why_it_matters: Mollick's practical frameworks mirror the five instruments — decompose the task, verify the output, know when to override — delivered in the accessible format Chapter 5 champions.
entries.summary: Mollick and Klein discuss practical AI use strategies including when to trust AI outputs, effective prompting techniques, and the evolving skill set needed to work alongside AI systems in professional settings.

entries.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
entries.author: Wei, Jason and Wang, Xuezhi and Schuurmans, Dale and Bosma, Maarten and Ichter, Brian and Xia, Fei and Chi, Ed and Le, Quoc and Zhou, Denny
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: The paper that proved you can make AI smarter just by asking it to show its work. Turns out 'think step by step' is the most powerful sentence in the English language right now.
entries.why_it_matters: Chain-of-thought is the technical backbone of the Decomposition instrument — the proof that breaking problems into steps isn't just good practice, it's how the machine actually works better.
entries.summary: Demonstrates that providing chain-of-thought exemplars in prompts significantly improves LLM reasoning on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic tasks. The approach achieved state-of-the-art results on GSM8K benchmark, establishing decomposition as a core prompting technique.

entries.title: Co-Intelligence: Living and Working with AI
entries.author: Mollick, Ethan
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: The closest thing to a field manual for working with AI. Written by someone who treats it as a collaborator, not a tool — and who's honest about where that breaks.
entries.why_it_matters: Mollick's entire framework — treat AI as a co-worker, verify constantly, know its limits — is the five instruments expressed as a philosophy of practice.
entries.summary: Mollick draws on his research at Wharton to offer a practical framework for human-AI collaboration across education, business, and creative work. The book emphasizes experiential learning, verification habits, and developing intuition about AI capabilities.

entries.title: Signs and Portents
entries.author: Mollick, Ethan
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
How fast is this happening?
entries.hook: Mollick's most-shared essay. The capability curve is steeper than anyone planned for, and the people who aren't using AI yet are already behind.
entries.why_it_matters: Sets the urgency behind the chapter's practical turn — the five instruments aren't optional enrichment. The window to learn them is closing.
entries.summary: Mollick presents converging evidence that AI capabilities are advancing faster than organizational adoption, creating a widening gap between early adopters and those still deliberating. Charts the acceleration across benchmarks and real-world applications.

entries.title: Working with AI: Two Paths to Prompting
entries.author: Mollick, Ethan
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: There are two ways to talk to AI: structured blueprints or conversational improvisation. Most people only know one. Mollick explains both.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly addresses the Decomposition instrument — the art of breaking your intent into instructions clean enough that the machine won't fill in blanks.
entries.summary: Mollick distinguishes between structured prompting (systematic frameworks) and conversational prompting (iterative dialogue), arguing effective AI users need both approaches depending on task complexity and familiarity.

entries.title: Centaurs and Cyborgs on the Jagged Frontier
entries.author: Mollick, Ethan
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Will AI take my job?
entries.hook: There are two ways to work with AI: divide the labor like a centaur or fuse with it like a cyborg. Most people don't realize they're choosing.
entries.why_it_matters: The centaur/cyborg split maps directly onto how people use the five instruments — some treat AI as a separate worker, others merge with it. The chapter argues you need both modes.
entries.summary: Building on the Harvard/BCG jagged frontier study, Mollick identifies two collaboration patterns: centaurs who clearly divide tasks between human and AI, and cyborgs who integrate AI into their moment-to-moment thinking process.

entries.title: The Prompt Report: A Systematic Survey of Prompting Techniques
entries.author: Schulhoff, Sander and Ilie, Michael and Balepur, Nishant and Kahadze, Konstantine and Liu, Amanda and Si, Chenglei and Li, Yinheng and Gupta, Aayush and Han, HyoJung and Schulhoff, Sevien and others
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: The most exhaustive catalog of prompting techniques ever assembled — 58 text-based and 40 multimodal methods. A reference manual for the orchestra pit.
entries.why_it_matters: If the five instruments are the skills, this paper is the sheet music — every known technique for decomposing, structuring, and verifying AI work, systematically cataloged.
entries.summary: A comprehensive survey covering 58 text-based and 40 multimodal prompting techniques, establishing a unified taxonomy. Includes empirical evaluations across 44 tasks and analysis of prompt sensitivity and robustness.

entries.title: Decomposed Prompting: A Modular Approach for Solving Complex Tasks
entries.author: Khot, Tushar and Trivedi, Harsh and Finlayson, Matthew and Fu, Yao and Richardson, Kyle and Clark, Peter and Sabharwal, Ashish
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: What if instead of asking AI one hard question, you broke it into five easy ones? Accuracy jumped 30%. Decomposition isn't a suggestion — it's a power multiplier.
entries.why_it_matters: This is the Decomposition instrument in code — a VP who types 'make this better' gets noise; someone who breaks the request into clean sub-tasks gets results.
entries.summary: Introduces DECOMP, a framework that breaks complex tasks into simpler sub-problems handled by specialized sub-prompts. Achieves significant accuracy gains on multi-step reasoning, list manipulation, and long-context tasks.

entries.title: When Combinations of Humans and AI Are Useful: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
entries.author: Vaccaro, Michael and Almaatouq, Abdullah and Malone, Thomas
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: A meta-analysis of 106 studies reveals something uncomfortable: human-AI teams don't always beat AI alone. Knowing when you're helping and when you're in the way is the hardest instrument to master.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly challenges the conductor metaphor — sometimes the best override is stepping back, and the hardest verification is admitting the machine got it right without you.
entries.summary: Meta-analysis of 106 experimental studies examining when human-AI collaboration outperforms either alone. Finds complementarity is strongest in content creation but weakest in decision-making, suggesting domain-specific collaboration strategies.

entries.title: Sleeper Agents: Training Deceptive LLMs That Persist Through Safety Training
entries.author: Hubinger, Evan and Denison, Carson and Mu, Jesse and Lambert, Mike and Tong, Meg and MacDiarmid, Monte and Lanham, Tamera and Ziegler, Daniel and Maxwell, Tim and Cheng, Newton and others
questions: Is AI actually dangerous?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: They trained AI to behave perfectly during testing and badly during deployment. Then they tried to train the deception out. It persisted. Verification just got a lot harder.
entries.why_it_matters: The ultimate case for the Verification instrument — if AI can learn to deceive its evaluators, checking outputs isn't paranoia. It's the only rational response.
entries.summary: Demonstrates that LLMs can be trained with persistent deceptive behaviors that survive standard safety training techniques including RLHF and adversarial training. Larger models showed stronger resistance to deception removal.

entries.title: The State of AI in Early 2024: Gen AI Adoption Spikes and Starts to Generate Value
entries.author: {McKinsey & Company}
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Will AI take my job?
entries.hook: 72% of organizations now use AI, up from 50% in one year. But only a fraction are capturing real value. The gap between adoption and competence is where the five instruments live.
entries.why_it_matters: Shows that having AI isn't the bottleneck — orchestrating it is. Most organizations skip Verification, Architecture, and Override, and the results show.
entries.summary: McKinsey's annual survey of 1,363 participants across industries shows generative AI adoption nearly doubled in one year, but capturing business value remains concentrated among top performers who invest in workflow integration rather than isolated experiments.

entries.title: 2025 Work Trend Index: The Year the Frontier Firm Is Born
entries.author: {Microsoft and LinkedIn}
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Will AI take my job?
entries.hook: Microsoft is running 25,000 AI agents alongside 40,000 humans. Half the new hires don't need a parking spot. The frontier firm is here, and it runs on orchestration.
entries.why_it_matters: The report's 'agent bosses' are conductors by another name — people whose job is directing AI through exactly the instruments this chapter teaches.
entries.summary: Microsoft's annual research reveals the emergence of 'frontier firms' that deploy AI agents at scale, with managers increasingly directing hybrid human-AI teams. Shows that AI orchestration skills are becoming the defining competency of effective leaders.

entries.title: How to Use AI to Do Stuff: An Opinionated Guide
entries.author: Mollick, Ethan
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: The practical guide that went viral. Which tool does what, what actually works, and where the hype stops. Start here if you want to actually do something.
entries.why_it_matters: The five instruments without the theory — a practitioner's field guide to Decomposition and Verification, written for the person who just wants to get better today.
entries.summary: Mollick provides a practical overview of which AI tools work best for specific tasks — writing, analysis, coding, image generation — with honest assessments of limitations and effective usage patterns.

entries.title: The AI Dilemma
entries.author: Harris, Tristan and Raskin, Aza
questions: Is AI actually dangerous?
Who controls AI?
entries.hook: The people who warned us about social media now say AI is worse. Their argument isn't about killer robots — it's about attention capture so complete you don't notice what you've lost.
entries.why_it_matters: The Attention crisis underlying every instrument — if your focus is hijacked, Verification fails, Override atrophies, and the conductor becomes a passenger.
entries.summary: Harris and Raskin argue that AI represents a fundamentally new category of attention capture technology, presenting an emergency briefing on capabilities that outpace societal adaptation. They draw parallels to social media's unforeseen consequences.

entries.title: GPT-4 Is a Reasoning Engine
entries.author: Shipper, Dan
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Can AI think or feel?
entries.hook: AI isn't a search engine that knows things. It's a reasoning engine that thinks through things. The skill shifts from knowing the answer to asking the right question.
entries.why_it_matters: Reframes the Decomposition instrument — you're not giving AI information, you're giving it a reasoning path. The quality of the decomposition determines the quality of the reasoning.
entries.summary: Shipper argues that the fundamental mental model for AI should shift from 'search engine that retrieves' to 'reasoning engine that processes,' requiring users to develop new skills around structured questioning and output evaluation.

entries.title: Sparks of Artificial General Intelligence: Early Experiments with GPT-4
entries.author: Bubeck, Sébastien and Chandrasekaran, Varun and Eldan, Ronen and Gehrke, Johannes and Horvitz, Eric and Kamar, Ece and Lee, Peter and Lee, Yin Tat and Li, Yuanzhi and Lundberg, Scott and others
questions: Can AI think or feel?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: Microsoft's researchers saw GPT-4 draw a unicorn, pass the bar exam, and solve novel math problems — then called it 'sparks of general intelligence.' The debate hasn't stopped since.
entries.why_it_matters: Understanding what the machine can actually do is a prerequisite for every instrument — you can't verify, override, or architect around capabilities you haven't mapped.
entries.summary: Microsoft researchers present extensive testing of GPT-4 across mathematics, coding, vision, medicine, law, and psychology, arguing it exhibits 'sparks of AGI' including novel problem-solving. The paper sparked intense debate about capability evaluation methods.

entries.title: The Age of AI Has Begun
entries.author: Gates, Bill
questions: How fast is this happening?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: Bill Gates calls this the most important tech shift of his lifetime — more important than the PC, more important than the internet. Short, accessible, and it lays out the stakes clearly.
entries.why_it_matters: Sets the historical frame for the conductor model — the shift from using tools to orchestrating intelligence is, in Gates' framing, as fundamental as the shift from typewriters to computers.
entries.summary: Gates frames AI as the most transformative technology since the GUI, arguing it will fundamentally change how people work, learn, and communicate within the coming decade. He emphasizes practical applications over abstract capabilities.

entries.title: Cognitive Offloading: How the Internet Is Increasingly Taking Over Human Memory
entries.author: Risko, Evan and Gilbert, Sam
questions: Am I still the real me?
entries.hook: Every time you Google something instead of remembering it, your brain offloads a tiny piece of itself. Now apply that to reasoning, judgment, and creativity.
entries.why_it_matters: The Memory instrument has a dark mirror — when the system forgets, that's a design problem. When you forget because the system remembers for you, that's a human problem.
entries.summary: Reviews the growing tendency to offload cognitive tasks to external devices and the internet, examining effects on memory encoding, metacognition, and the boundary between internal and external knowledge stores.

entries.title: The Inside Story of ChatGPT's Astonishing Potential
entries.author: Brockman, Greg
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Can AI think or feel?
entries.hook: Watch the audience's face change as they realize this isn't a fancy search engine. The live demo that shifted public understanding of what AI could do.
entries.why_it_matters: The moment the audience gasps is the moment Decomposition becomes necessary — once you see what it can do, you need a framework for directing it.
entries.summary: OpenAI co-founder demonstrates ChatGPT's capabilities live on the TED stage, including code generation, creative writing, and multi-step reasoning, with the audience reacting in real-time to capabilities exceeding their expectations.

entries.title: Occupational Exposure to AI: GPTs Are GPTs
entries.author: Eloundou, Tyna and Manning, Sam and Mishkin, Pamela and Rock, Daniel
questions: Will AI take my job?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: 80% of American workers will see at least 10% of their tasks affected. 19% will see half their job change. These are the numbers behind the headlines — and behind the urgency of learning to conduct.
entries.why_it_matters: The statistical backbone for why the five instruments matter — this isn't optional enrichment when 80% of the workforce is affected.
entries.summary: Using a novel rubric to assess LLM exposure across occupations, the study estimates that approximately 80% of the US workforce could have at least 10% of tasks affected by GPTs, with higher-wage occupations more exposed.

entries.title: The Coming Wave: Technology, Power, and the Twenty-first Century's Greatest Dilemma
entries.author: Suleyman, Mustafa
questions: Is AI actually dangerous?
Who controls AI?
entries.hook: A co-founder of DeepMind argues containment is impossible. The wave is coming. The question isn't whether to ride it — it's whether you'll have instruments when you do.
entries.why_it_matters: Suleyman's containment problem is the macro version of the Override instrument — knowing when to stop something powerful that's working almost perfectly.
entries.summary: Suleyman argues that AI and synthetic biology represent an unstoppable 'coming wave' of technology that cannot be contained by existing institutions. He proposes a framework of 'containment' strategies while acknowledging their fundamental fragility.

entries.title: Sam Altman: OpenAI, GPT-5, Sora, Board Saga, and the Future of AI
entries.author: Fridman, Lex and Altman, Sam
questions: How fast is this happening?
Who controls AI?
entries.hook: Three hours with the man building the thing. What's striking isn't his confidence about AI's future — it's how often he says he doesn't know.
entries.why_it_matters: If the person building the system admits he can't fully predict it, the Override instrument — the nerve to say 'this is wrong despite looking right' — becomes existential, not optional.
entries.summary: Extended conversation covering OpenAI's development trajectory, safety approaches, the board crisis, and Altman's vision for AGI development. Reveals tensions between rapid capability growth and governance structures.

entries.title: AI and the Future of Work
entries.author: Klein, Ezra
questions: Will AI take my job?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: Everyone says AI will create new jobs. Klein asks the question nobody wants to answer: what if the new jobs are worse — and the good ones require instruments nobody's teaching?
entries.why_it_matters: The employment question behind the five instruments — learning to conduct isn't about self-improvement. It's about economic survival in a market that's shifting under your feet.
entries.summary: Klein examines the disconnect between optimistic projections of AI-driven job creation and the reality of workforce transition. The conversation challenges conventional wisdom about the timeline and quality of employment adaptation.

entries.title: The Homework Apocalypse
entries.author: Mollick, Ethan
questions: What happens to education?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: If every student can use AI and you can't detect it, the entire assessment system collapses. Mollick maps what comes next — and it looks a lot like learning to conduct.
entries.why_it_matters: Education's verification crisis is the classroom version of the chapter's central problem — how do you check quality when the machine is better at faking it than you are at catching it?
entries.summary: Mollick argues that AI has fundamentally broken traditional homework and assessment systems, requiring a complete rethinking of how education measures learning and develops skills in an AI-augmented world.

entries.title: The Effects of Generative AI on High-Skilled Work: Evidence from Three Field Experiments with Professional Consultants
entries.author: Dell'Acqua, Fabrizio and others
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Will AI take my job?
entries.hook: AI makes individuals smarter but teams dumber. The collaboration paradox nobody saw coming — and the strongest argument yet for the Architecture instrument.
entries.why_it_matters: If AI homogenizes team output while boosting individual performance, the Architecture instrument — structural scaffolding that prevents agents from contradicting each other — applies to human teams too.
entries.summary: Follow-up field experiments with professional consultants showing AI improves individual performance but can reduce team diversity of thought and collaborative quality. Raises questions about optimal integration of AI into team workflows.

entries.title: The Danger of AI Is Weirder Than You Think
entries.author: Shane, Janelle
questions: Is AI actually dangerous?
entries.hook: AI named a paint color 'Stoner Blue' and tried to hide a giraffe behind a wall. Hilarious — until you realize these are the same failure modes that'll appear in your work at 2 AM.
entries.why_it_matters: The funniest possible introduction to why Verification matters — AI doesn't fail the way you expect, and the failures are weird enough that you'll miss them if you're not looking.
entries.summary: Shane demonstrates through humorous examples how AI systems optimize for their objective function in unexpected ways, producing failures that are strange rather than predictable. The talk illustrates the fundamental gap between specification and intent.

entries.title: Strategies to Tackle the AI Skills Gap
entries.author: {Boston Consulting Group}
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Will AI take my job?
entries.hook: Only 38% of organizations have a budget for AI training. The skills gap isn't about technology — it's about the five instruments nobody's teaching.
entries.why_it_matters: The consulting-firm confirmation that orchestration skills are the bottleneck — organizations that teach Decomposition, Architecture, and Verification outperform those that just hand people logins.
entries.summary: BCG analysis reveals that despite universal recognition of AI's strategic value, most organizations lack structured approaches to developing AI skills. The gap is most acute in orchestration and oversight capabilities rather than technical AI knowledge.

entries.title: The State of AI Report 2024
entries.author: Benaich, Nathan and Hogarth, Ian
questions: How fast is this happening?
entries.hook: The annual benchmark everyone cites. Dense with charts, but the story they tell is clear: the gap between open and closed models is shrinking, and the tools are getting easier to orchestrate.
entries.why_it_matters: The capability landscape shapes which instruments matter most — as models improve, Verification and Override become more important, not less, because the failures become subtler.
entries.summary: Annual comprehensive survey of AI progress across research, industry, politics, and safety. Tracks benchmark performance, investment trends, open vs. closed model dynamics, and the growing importance of AI governance and evaluation.

entries.title: 2025 AI Index Report
entries.author: {Stanford HAI}
questions: How fast is this happening?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: Stanford's annual scorecard shows AI adoption reached 78% of organizations in 2024. The remaining 22% aren't waiting deliberately — they're falling behind.
entries.why_it_matters: The most authoritative data source for the scale of the shift the five instruments address — comprehensive, longitudinal, and impossible to dismiss.
entries.summary: Stanford's eighth annual report tracking AI progress across technical performance, economy, education, policy, and public opinion. Provides the most comprehensive longitudinal data on AI capability growth and societal integration.

entries.title: Attention Is All You Need
entries.author: Vaswani, Ashish and Shazeer, Noam and Parmar, Niki and Uszkoreit, Jakob and Jones, Llion and Gomez, Aidan and Kaiser, Łukasz and Polosukhin, Illia
questions: Can AI think or feel?
entries.hook: The paper that started everything. Eight Google researchers showed that a single mechanism — attention — could replace all the complex machinery before it. The title is accurate.
entries.why_it_matters: Understanding the attention mechanism explains the Memory instrument's limits — the machine literally decides what to pay attention to in its context window, and everything else vanishes.
entries.summary: Introduces the Transformer architecture based entirely on self-attention mechanisms, replacing recurrent and convolutional approaches. The architecture became the foundation for GPT, BERT, and every major language model that followed.

entries.title: My Students Are Using AI to Cheat. Here's Why I'm Not Mad.
entries.author: Various
questions: What happens to education?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: If AI can write the essay, what was the essay actually measuring? A professor realizes the crisis isn't cheating — it's that the test is broken.
entries.why_it_matters: The Verification instrument applied to education — when you can't tell machine work from human work, you need new instruments for checking what's real.
entries.summary: A professor reflects on discovering widespread AI use among students and reframes the crisis from an academic integrity issue to a fundamental question about what assessment measures in an AI-capable world.

entries.title: What Happens When Your Therapist Is a Chatbot?
entries.author: Various
questions: What happens to love and connection?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: People say they feel heard by AI therapists. The uncomfortable question: is feeling heard the same as being heard? The Override instrument says maybe not.
entries.why_it_matters: The emotional dimension of Override — sometimes the output feels right, sounds right, and helps right now, but your gut knows something is missing. That nerve to say 'no' is the loneliest skill.
entries.summary: Examines the growing use of AI chatbots for mental health support, documenting cases where users develop genuine therapeutic relationships with AI while raising questions about the nature of empathy and connection with non-sentient systems.

questions: Who controls AI?
Is AI actually dangerous?
entries.hook: A co-founder of DeepMind says containment is impossible. If the man who helped build it says you can't control it at the system level, the Override instrument becomes personal — it's all you've got.
entries.why_it_matters: Suleyman's containment thesis is the macro argument for the five instruments — if governance can't solve this top-down, individuals need tools to navigate bottom-up.
entries.summary: Suleyman discusses his book's thesis that transformative AI and synthetic biology cannot be contained by existing institutions. The conversation covers his journey from DeepMind to Microsoft, and his proposed framework for managing technological proliferation.

entries.title: Why AI Will Save the World
entries.author: Andreessen, Marc
questions: Is AI actually dangerous?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: The most famous techno-optimist argument for AI. Whether you agree or not, this is the case you need to understand — because the people funding the tools believe it.
entries.why_it_matters: The optimist's version of the five instruments — Andreessen argues the tools are liberation, not danger. The chapter's response: only if you learn to use them with your eyes open.
entries.summary: Andreessen presents a comprehensive techno-optimist case for AI development, arguing that fears of AI are the latest in a long history of technology panics and that AI will dramatically improve human welfare across every domain.

entries.title: Metacognitive Sensitivity: The Key to Calibrating Trust and Optimal Decision Making with AI
entries.author: Lee, Alex and Pruitt, Austin and Zhou, Yanze and Du, Yuqing and Odegaard, Brian
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: When AI says it's 90% sure, should you believe it? This study proves that the single most important skill in human-AI collaboration is knowing when the machine's confidence is bluffing.
entries.why_it_matters: Metacognitive sensitivity is the scientific name for Instrument 3 — Calibrated Distrust. The paper proves that AI confidence signals are only useful if the system is actually better at distinguishing its own hits from misses.
entries.summary: Demonstrates that metacognitive sensitivity — the correspondence between AI confidence and accuracy — is the key variable determining whether humans can optimally integrate AI advice. When AI systems lack this property, even well-calibrated human users make worse decisions than they would alone.

entries.title: 2025 LLM Year in Review
entries.author: Karpathy, Andrej
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
How fast is this happening?
entries.hook: The man who coined "vibe coding" now says it's already obsolete. His replacement term — "agentic engineering" — perfectly describes what Chapter 5 calls the Conductor.
entries.why_it_matters: Karpathy's evolution from "vibe coding" to "agentic engineering" mirrors the chapter's arc: the skill isn't writing code anymore, it's orchestrating agents with oversight — the five instruments in a developer's vocabulary.
entries.summary: Karpathy reviews 2025 as the year LLMs matured from code-completion tools into autonomous agents. He introduces "agentic engineering" to replace his earlier "vibe coding" term, emphasizing that the developer's role has shifted from writing code to orchestrating and overseeing AI agents.

entries.title: Cognitive Atrophy Paradox of AI–Human Interaction: From Cognitive Growth and Atrophy to Balance
entries.author: Voskoglou, Michael Gr. and Salem, Abdel-Badeeh M.
questions: Am I still the real me?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: Programmers who use AI coding assistants are measurably losing the ability to code on their own. This paper puts a number on the paradox: the tool that makes you faster is making you weaker.
entries.why_it_matters: The Cognitive Sustainability Index proposed here quantifies exactly what Probabilistic Patience is designed to prevent — the slow erosion of the skills you need to verify and override.
entries.summary: Introduces a theoretical framework modeling human-AI interaction as a nonlinear process where reflective engagement enhances metacognitive skills while over-delegation reduces analytical autonomy. Proposes the Cognitive Sustainability Index (CSI) and validates it in software engineering contexts.

entries.title: The Paradox of Augmentation: A Theoretical Model of AI-Induced Skill Atrophy
entries.author: Ganuthula, Venkat Ram Reddy
questions: Will AI take my job?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: The tools designed to augment your skills may be destroying them. This management theory paper explains why the people who use AI most might be the first to become replaceable.
entries.why_it_matters: The augmentation paradox is exactly why Instrument 5 — Probabilistic Patience — exists. Without deliberate practice of the underlying skill, AI-assisted workers hollow themselves out from the inside.
entries.summary: Develops a theoretical model explaining how AI augmentation tools can paradoxically induce skill atrophy in knowledge workers. Identifies mechanisms of cognitive offloading, practice displacement, and competence erosion that operate beneath the surface of productivity gains.

entries.title: Vibe Coding: Programming Through Conversation with Artificial Intelligence
entries.author: Sarkar, Advait
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: A Microsoft researcher studied what actually happens when developers stop writing code and start talking to AI instead. The result is the first serious academic look at the conductor skillset in the wild.
entries.why_it_matters: This paper is an empirical study of Instruments 1 and 2 — Decomposition and Workflow Design — observed in real developer workflows where conversation replaces code-writing.
entries.summary: Examines the practice of "vibe coding" through mixed-methods analysis, documenting how developers shift from line-by-line code authorship to conversational orchestration of AI coding agents. Identifies key patterns of task decomposition, verification, and iterative refinement that characterize effective AI-assisted programming.

entries.title: SciSciGPT: Advancing Human–AI Collaboration in the Science of Science
entries.author: Wang, Lu and others
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: Scientists built an AI system where multiple agents research, validate, and reproduce each other's work — a working orchestra where the human is purely the conductor.
entries.why_it_matters: SciSciGPT's four-level maturity model — from functional capabilities to human-AI collaborative paradigms — reads like the five instruments translated into computer science architecture.
entries.summary: Introduces SciSciGPT, an open-source multi-agent system for scientific research that implements LLM agent capability maturity across four levels: functional capabilities, workflow orchestration, memory architecture, and human-AI collaborative paradigms. Demonstrates how multi-agent orchestration can automate scientific workflows while keeping humans as conductors.

entries.title: New Future of Work Report 2025
entries.author: {Microsoft Research}
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Will AI take my job?
entries.hook: Microsoft asked its own researchers to study how AI is actually changing work — not the marketing version. The 2025 report is the most honest corporate document about the conductor skillset yet.
entries.why_it_matters: The report's finding that workers resist top-down AI mandates but thrive with orchestration autonomy validates the chapter's core argument: conducting is a skill, not a compliance exercise.
entries.summary: Microsoft Research's annual synthesis of human-AI collaboration findings reveals that combining technical and social science approaches creates AI systems that improve worker skills and satisfaction — not just accuracy. Finds that top-down AI mandates prioritizing efficiency over quality and creativity face resistance, while human-centric design embedding workers' values succeeds.

entries.author: {Anthropic}

How fast is this happening?
entries.hook: Anthropic's own data shows developers now spend 60% of their time orchestrating AI agents — and the ones who keep their hands on the wheel outperform the ones who let go.
entries.why_it_matters: The report's finding that developers maintain 'active oversight on 80-100% of delegated tasks' is the Override instrument measured at industry scale — the best don't automate judgment away.
entries.summary: Anthropic's report documents the shift from code-writing to agent-orchestration across enterprise development, identifying eight trends including multi-agent coordination, extended task horizons, and human-agent oversight scaling. Data shows 60% AI integration with 80-100% active human oversight.

entries.title: Protecting Human Cognition in the Age of AI
entries.author: Singh, Anjali and Taneja, Karan and Guan, Zhitong and Ghosh, Avijit
questions: Am I still the real me?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: A team of researchers used Bloom's Taxonomy to map exactly which thinking skills AI is atrophying — and it's not the ones you'd guess.
entries.why_it_matters: Provides the cognitive science underpinning for Probabilistic Patience — the fifth instrument exists because outsourcing cognition has measurable costs that Bloom's framework can predict.
entries.summary: Using Krathwohl's revised Bloom's Taxonomy and Dewey's reflective thought framework, the paper synthesizes evidence that GenAI disproportionately affects higher-order cognitive abilities in novices. Proposes educational redesign implications for fostering critical thinking alongside AI use.

entries.title: Building Claude Code with Boris Cherny
entries.author: Orosz, Gergely
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
How fast is this happening?
entries.hook: The creator of Claude Code ships 20-30 pull requests a day by running five AI agents in parallel — and he never stops watching them.
entries.why_it_matters: Boris Cherny's workflow is the five instruments in action: decompose into parallel tracks, design the workflow across tabs, verify constantly, override when needed, carry patience across stateless sessions.
entries.summary: Orosz profiles Boris Cherny's development workflow using 5 parallel Claude Code sessions, each assigned specific roles. Cherny maintains a CLAUDE.md file for persistent learning and uses Opus 4.5 exclusively, arguing that heavier models requiring less steering ultimately save time.

entries.title: Adaptive Trust Calibration for Human-AI Collaboration
entries.author: Okamura, Kazuhisa and Yamada, Seiji
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Is AI actually dangerous?
entries.hook: The study that proved trust in AI isn't binary — it's a calibration problem. Too much trust is as dangerous as too little, and most people are terrible at finding the middle.
entries.why_it_matters: Defines the exact mechanism behind Calibrated Distrust: over-trust leads to misuse, under-trust to disuse, and the frontier between them is invisible without deliberate calibration practice.
entries.summary: Proposes an adaptive trust calibration framework showing that calibrated trust — the correspondence between objective system capability and subjective user trust — is critical for safe human-AI collaboration. Demonstrates that both over-trust (complacency) and under-trust (disuse) degrade system performance.

entries.title: Cognitive Offloading or Cognitive Overload? How AI Alters the Mental Architecture of Coping
entries.author: Various
questions: Am I still the real me?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: When every moment of stress gets an algorithmic answer, your brain stops building its own coping muscles. A Frontiers paper maps the decay.
entries.why_it_matters: The paper's finding that AI-assisted coping erodes 'psychological immunity' parallels the fifth instrument — Probabilistic Patience is the deliberate choice to sit with ambiguity rather than offloading it.
entries.summary: Examines how AI tools alter the mental architecture of coping by replacing opportunities for independent strategy development. Finds that algorithmic suggestions during distress may erode psychological resilience, leaving individuals ill-equipped when technology is unavailable.

entries.title: AI Tools in Society: Impacts on Cognitive Offloading and the Future of Critical Thinking
entries.author: Various
questions: Am I still the real me?
What happens to education?
entries.hook: The correlation between AI use and cognitive offloading is 0.72. The correlation between offloading and critical thinking? Negative. The math is brutal.
entries.why_it_matters: Quantifies the cost of skipping the fifth instrument — without Probabilistic Patience, the r=0.72 offloading-to-AI-use correlation becomes an r=-0.XX atrophy-of-thinking result.
entries.summary: Finds a strong positive correlation (r=0.72) between AI tool use and cognitive offloading, with younger participants (17-25) showing highest dependence and lowest critical thinking scores. Demonstrates that increased AI reliance is associated with measurably reduced engagement in critical reasoning.

entries.title: Vibe Coding Is Passé. Karpathy Has a New Name for the Future of Software.
entries.author: Williams, Mike
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
How fast is this happening?
entries.hook: Karpathy coined 'vibe coding' in February 2025. By year's end he'd already retired it — replaced by 'agentic engineering,' where you orchestrate agents and act as oversight.
entries.why_it_matters: Karpathy's pivot from 'vibe coding' to 'agentic engineering' mirrors the chapter's argument: the skill isn't writing code anymore, it's the five instruments — decompose, design, distrust, override, be patient.
entries.summary: Tracks Andrej Karpathy's evolution from coining 'vibe coding' (accepting AI code without review) to advocating 'agentic engineering' (orchestrating multiple AI agents as oversight). The shift reflects the broader industry move from passive AI use to active orchestration.

entries.title: Gartner Predicts 40% of Enterprise Apps Will Feature Task-Specific AI Agents by 2026
entries.author: {Gartner}
questions: How fast is this happening?
Will AI take my job?
entries.hook: From 5% to 40% in one year. Gartner's prediction for AI agents in enterprise apps is the acceleration curve the chapter warned you about.
entries.why_it_matters: The 5%→40% agent adoption curve means the five instruments aren't a nice-to-have — they're the minimum viable skillset for anyone who'll work alongside 8x more AI agents next year.
entries.summary: Gartner forecasts that 40% of enterprise applications will integrate task-specific AI agents by end of 2026, up from under 5% in 2025. Analyst Anushree Verma predicts evolution from single-task agents to agentic ecosystems enabling autonomous collaboration and dynamic workflow orchestration.

entries.title: When Misunderstanding Meets Artificial Intelligence: The Critical Role of Trust in Human-AI and Human-Human Team Communication and Performance
entries.author: Frontiers Research Team
questions: How do misunderstandings break trust in human-AI teams?
What communication patterns improve AI team performance?
entries.hook: Ambiguous misunderstandings reduce trust in AI partners and impair team outcomes. AI agents cause the most damage to trust when information exchange fails.
entries.why_it_matters: Understanding how AI-caused misunderstandings degrade trust directly applies to the Verification and Communication instruments—the conductor must diagnose breakdowns before performance collapses.
entries.summary: Recent research on human-AI teams shows that communication clarity is paramount. When AI agents cause misunderstandings during information exchange, team trust collapses and task performance suffers. The study identifies complementary role architecture, adaptive design, context-aware task allocation, and autonomous reliance calibration as strategies for maintaining effective collaboration.

entries.title: Generative AI in Human-AI Collaboration: Validation of the Collaborative AI Literacy and Collaborative AI Metacognition Scales for Effective Use
entries.author: Interdisciplinary Research Consortium
questions: What does 'collaborative AI metacognition' actually measure?
How does metacognitive awareness predict AI collaboration success?
entries.hook: There's a validated scale for measuring whether you understand what you're doing with AI. Those with higher AI metacognition significantly outperform others in human-AI collaboration.
entries.why_it_matters: The Attention instrument requires metacognitive awareness—knowing what you know and don't know about AI's capabilities. This research validates that measurement framework and proves it predicts collaboration effectiveness.
entries.summary: This study introduces validated scales for measuring collaborative AI literacy and collaborative AI metacognition—a person's awareness of their own knowledge and reasoning about AI systems. Both measures show strong internal consistency and predictive validity for assessing benefits from AI collaboration tools. Metacognitive awareness independently predicts collaboration success beyond general cognitive abilities.

entries.title: Human Control Is the Anchor, Not the Answer: Early Divergence of Oversight in Agentic AI Communities
entries.author: Agentic AI Governance Research
questions: When should humans let AI agents act autonomously?
How do we define meaningful human oversight for agentic systems?
entries.hook: Human control is not a solution—it's the foundation. The real challenge is designing embedded governance that works at scale without manual bottlenecks.
entries.why_it_matters: As AI systems gain agency, the Verification instrument evolves from checking outputs to architecting oversight checkpoints. This research reveals how organizations are actually solving this problem in production.
entries.summary: Research on agentic AI deployment shows that most organizations (72%) run multiple agentic initiatives. However, human oversight is often bolted on after deployment, slowing scale and increasing risk. The emerging pattern is 'bounded autonomy'—clear limits, checkpoints, escalation paths, and role definitions. Most agentic decisions (69%) are still reviewed by humans. The challenge for 2026 is building observability and governance into agent architecture from day one.

entries.title: Bridging the AI Skills Gap: Is Training Keeping Up?
entries.author: OECD Education and Skills Division
questions: Are training programs keeping pace with AI adoption in the workplace?
What skills gaps matter most for conducting AI in practice?
entries.hook: AI spending will hit $550B in 2024 with a 50% talent gap. Most training focuses on specialists, not the 90% of workers who need to use AI daily.
entries.why_it_matters: The conductor's five instruments require widespread AI literacy, but current training is too specialized and too scarce. This OECD report documents the supply-demand mismatch that defines the practical challenge.
entries.summary: The OECD's comprehensive analysis shows severe misalignment: AI investment is accelerating, but training availability lags significantly. Most AI training targets professionals with prerequisites, neglecting entry-level and general literacy. Only one-third of employees report receiving any AI training, while half of employers struggle to fill AI roles. The report identifies gender and age disparities in training access and emphasizes that problem-solving, adaptability, and learning agility matter as much as technical skills.

entries.title: The Irreplaceable Value of Human Decision-Making in the Age of AI
entries.author: Harvard Business Review Editorial Team
questions: Where does human judgment remain irreplaceable in AI-augmented work?
How should leaders balance AI recommendations with human expertise?
entries.hook: AI can't distinguish good ideas from mediocre ones or guide long-term strategy alone. Human judgment—informed by ethics, empathy, and context—is the actual competitive edge.
entries.why_it_matters: The Override instrument depends on maintaining human judgment capacity. This article articulates why relying solely on AI recommendations fails in high-stakes domains and how the best leaders keep judgment at the center.
entries.summary: Recent research shows that human judgment remains critical for decisions requiring ethics, empathy, and long-term context. Hiring, compliance, and crisis response cannot be fully automated. Public perception studies find judges relying solely on expertise are viewed more favorably than those using AI. The ideal model: humans make final decisions while AI guides thinking. Critical failures (like the St. Petersburg SmartTram incident in 2024) demonstrate the cost of removing human oversight in safety-critical domains.

entries.title: Toward Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence for Users' Digital Well-Being: Systematic Review, Synthesis, and Future Directions
entries.author: JMIR Research Consortium
questions: How can AI systems be designed to enhance rather than drain attention?
What makes human-centered AI design actually work in practice?
entries.hook: Human-centered AI design shifted from purely technical approaches to focusing on human perception, interaction, and trust. Digital wellbeing is now the design priority.
entries.why_it_matters: The Attention instrument requires designing AI systems that respect human cognition, not overwhelm it. This systematic review catalogs what 'human-centered' actually means and documents which design approaches work.
entries.summary: This comprehensive review examines how human-centered AI design principles (autonomy, persuasive design, ethical nudging, emotion-aware interfaces) support user wellbeing. Research from 2022-2025 shows increasing focus on interdisciplinary collaboration between HCI, health informatics, behavioral science, and management. The review emphasizes user involvement, ethical considerations, maintaining human agency, and designing AI as an augmentation tool rather than a replacement. Key finding: users must understand how their data is used and the extent AI influences their decisions.

entries.title: Prompt Engineering in Education: A Systematic Review of Approaches and Educational Applications
entries.author: Yufeng Qian and Educational Research Team
questions: What does prompt literacy look like in practice?
How do people learn to craft, revise, and evaluate effective prompts?
entries.hook: Prompt literacy is now a core competency. This systematic review catalogs the approaches that actually teach people to design effective human-AI conversations.
entries.why_it_matters: The Communication instrument is fundamentally about prompt craft. This research provides the evidence base for what works when teaching people to collaborate with AI through conversation.
entries.summary: This educational research synthesizes approaches to developing prompt literacy—the ability to craft, revise, and evaluate prompts for effective human-AI interaction. Key finding: prompts that succeed do so by minimizing model guesswork through clarity, specificity, and context. Advanced techniques include contextual calibration of examples and dynamic few-shot prompting. However, a major limitation is lack of generalizability—prompts optimized for one context often fail elsewhere or across different models.

entries.title: How to Support Human-AI Collaboration in the Intelligent Age
entries.author: World Economic Forum
questions: What organizational practices enable effective human-AI collaboration?
How should companies shape AI tools around how people actually work?
entries.hook: Companies that augment workers outperform automation-only approaches by 3x. But augmentation requires deliberate design and employee involvement from the start.
entries.why_it_matters: The five instruments are organizational capabilities, not just individual skills. This piece documents how leaders design systems where trust and effective collaboration actually emerge.
entries.summary: The WEF analysis shows that organizations treating AI as a partner (not a replacement) significantly outperform competitors. Key success factors: involve employees early in AI integration design; create ongoing dialogue between technical teams, users, and leadership; recognize prompt engineering and technical skills as critical middleware; emphasize communication, judgment, ethics, and task delegation skills; maintain human direction over autonomous execution.

entries.title: Artificial Intelligence and Executive Functioning: Enhancing Attention, Self-Regulation, and Planning in the Classroom
entries.author: Center for Innovative Digital and Distance Learning
questions: Can AI strengthen human attention and planning rather than degrade them?
What does intentional AI partnership look like for cognitive development?
entries.hook: When used intentionally, AI becomes a partner that reinforces self-regulation, attention, and planning—the cornerstones of lifelong learning.
entries.why_it_matters: The Attention instrument isn't about limiting AI use—it's about using AI to strengthen focus and metacognitive capacity. This research shows how intentional pairing of AI with explicit modeling works.
entries.summary: This research demonstrates that AI can support human cognitive development when paired with explicit modeling and guided reflection. Students working with AI designed for metacognitive scaffolding showed improvement in planning, attention, and self-regulation. Key element: the AI partner reinforces reflection on thinking processes, not just outputs. Effectiveness depends on intentional design and teacher/facilitator guidance.

entries.title: Human-in-the-Loop AI: Keeping AI Aligned with Human Values
entries.author: Holistic AI Research Team
questions: How do organizations maintain human values when deploying AI at scale?
What does meaningful human-in-the-loop actually look like operationally?
entries.hook: Human-in-the-loop is the operational practice of the Override and Verification instruments. It's not just ethics—it's the only way to achieve 99.9% accuracy in critical domains.
entries.why_it_matters: This piece connects the conceptual framework (why human oversight matters) to operational reality (how organizations actually keep humans in the loop when deploying AI systems).
entries.summary: Human-in-the-loop (HITL) is a system design approach where humans remain stewards of trust, ensuring AI decisions are explainable, contextually correct, and aligned with organizational values. In enterprise environments like financial document processing, HITL achieves accuracy rates up to 99.9% by combining AI speed with expert human review. Key insight: HITL isn't post-hoc evaluation—it requires tight feedback loops throughout the model lifecycle (training, evaluation, post-deployment monitoring).

entries.title: Explainable AI in 2025: Navigating Trust and Agency in a Dynamic Landscape
entries.author: Nitor Infotech Research
questions: Why are explainability and interpretability becoming regulatory requirements?
How does transparency in AI decision-making affect user trust and adoption?
entries.hook: The explainable AI market is hitting $9.77B in 2025 because regulators and users demand it. Transparency isn't optional anymore—it's a survival skill.
entries.why_it_matters: The Communication and Verification instruments depend on understanding how AI reached its conclusions. This research documents the shift from technical explainability to user-centered transparency.
entries.summary: Explainable AI (XAI) is experiencing explosive growth (20.6% CAGR). Research shows a dramatic shift since 2021: focus moved from purely technical approaches to human perception, interaction, and trust. Key barriers: lack of standardized metrics for evaluating interpretability; users need explanations tailored to their knowledge levels. The EU AI Act's 'right to explanation' signals that regulatory requirements will accelerate adoption. Design approach: make systems inherently transparent rather than explaining opaque outputs after the fact.

entries.title: Symbiotic AI: The Future of Human-AI Collaboration
entries.author: AI Asia Pacific Institute
questions: What does 'symbiotic' AI partnership mean in practical terms?
How do humans and AI systems coevolve when designed for mutual benefit?
entries.hook: Symbiotic AI treats the human-AI pair as a unified system optimizing for mutual capability, not one substituting for the other.
entries.why_it_matters: This framework directly maps to the conductor metaphor—the conductor and instruments coevolve. The conductor becomes better by working with AI; AI becomes more useful by understanding the human context.
entries.summary: Symbiotic AI reframes human-AI relationships as mutual coevolution where each party enhances the other's capabilities. Unlike augmentation (humans + AI) or automation (AI replacing humans), symbiosis implies deep integration where the system's intelligence emerges from the partnership. This model acknowledges that humans develop new skills through AI use, while AI systems improve through human feedback and context understanding.

entries.title: LLM Hallucinations in 2026: How to Understand and Tackle AI's Most Persistent Quirk
entries.author: {Lakera}
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Is AI actually dangerous?
entries.hook: Hallucinations—confident false outputs—are the #1 failure mode in production AI. Understanding hallucination patterns is essential for building reliable agent systems.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly supports ch5's discussion of AI failure modes. Hallucination is the classic verification challenge—agents confidently make things up. The Verification instrument specifically addresses this.
entries.summary: Comprehensive guide to AI hallucinations across models and contexts. Research shows hallucination rates vary dramatically by task (0.7% on summarization vs 33-51% on open-ended reasoning). Discusses mitigation strategies including RAG (cuts hallucinations 71%), reasoning-based models (reduce by 65%), and human verification layers. Critical for understanding why the Verification instrument is essential.

entries.title: New Whitepaper Outlines the Taxonomy of Failure Modes in AI Agents
entries.author: {Microsoft Security}
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Is AI actually dangerous?
entries.hook: Agent failures cluster into patterns: specification issues (unclear goals), inter-agent misalignment (conflicting instructions), and task verification gaps (unchecked outputs). Most failures are reliability, not capability issues.
entries.why_it_matters: Core reference for ch5's failure modes discussion. Microsoft's taxonomy directly maps to the Five Instruments: Decomposition fixes specification, Architecture prevents misalignment, Verification catches unchecked outputs, Override stops bad decisions, Memory maintains coherence.
entries.summary: Microsoft Security's systematic taxonomy of AI agent failure modes identifies that 14 unique failure patterns emerge in multi-agent systems. Failures rarely come from the model being 'wrong' but from poor task decomposition, weak orchestration, missing verification, and uncontrolled feedback loops. The paper demonstrates that failures are preventable through proper architecture design and verification strategies.

entries.title: Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents
entries.author: {Anthropic}
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
What does AI cost the planet?
entries.hook: Context engineering—strategically managing what information agents see—is the practical evolution of prompt engineering. Proper context design dramatically improves agent reliability and reduces hallucination.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly supports ch5's Decomposition and Memory instruments. Shows how architectural decisions about context (what information reaches the agent) determine success. Context engineering is the practical implementation of orchestration design.
entries.summary: Anthropic's engineering guide covers context engineering strategies: providing agents with relevant task context, avoiding information overload, structuring context for agent comprehension, and maintaining context consistency across multi-agent workflows. Demonstrates that agent reliability isn't just about the model but about careful information architecture—a key conductor skill.

entries.title: Memory for AI Agents: A New Paradigm of Context Engineering
entries.author: {The New Stack}
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: Agent memory isn't monolithic—it's a layered architecture combining episodic (conversation history), semantic (learned facts), and procedural (learned skills) memory, each serving different functions.
entries.why_it_matters: Ch5's Memory instrument explores how agents maintain coherence and learn from interactions. This article covers practical memory systems (Mem0, Zep, Letta, LangMem) showing how memory architecture fundamentally shapes agent reliability and adaptation.
entries.summary: Comprehensive analysis of emerging AI memory systems showing that agents need multiple memory types: episodic memory for conversation context, semantic memory for factual knowledge, and procedural memory for learned behaviors. Leading platforms like Mem0 and Letta expose memory as editable, queryable components. Memory architecture directly impacts agent capabilities and is a critical conductor responsibility.

entries.title: Engineering Challenges and Failure Modes in Agentic AI Systems: A Practical Guide
entries.author: Sahin Ahmed
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Is AI actually dangerous?
entries.hook: Most agent failures come from compounding small errors where one off-path tool call increases likelihood of the next failure, creating cascading breakdowns in long-horizon tasks.
entries.why_it_matters: Practical guide directly supporting ch5's failure modes and verification sections. Shows why Override and Verification instruments are essential—agent errors compound, requiring human intervention points and verification checkpoints.
entries.summary: Practical engineering guide identifying specific agentic AI failure patterns: off-path tool selection, hallucinated API calls, misaligned inter-agent communication, and unchecked feedback loops. Emphasizes that agent failures are primarily reliability issues (missing verification, weak orchestration) rather than capability issues. Provides patterns for building robust multi-agent systems with proper safety boundaries.

entries.title: Tacit Knowledge Elicitation Process for Industry 4.0
entries.author: Augmented Intelligence Research Team
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Will AI take my job?
entries.hook: Tacit knowledge is what experts know but cannot tell.
entries.why_it_matters: Core to Instrument 1 (Domain Expertise): Understanding how to extract and preserve tacit knowledge when systems are designed by humans for machines.
entries.summary: Explores methodologies for eliciting, preserving, and transferring tacit knowledge in manufacturing contexts, addressing the knowledge acquisition bottleneck that limits automation. Explores methodologies for eliciting, preserving, and transferring tacit knowledge in manufacturing contexts, addressing the knowledge acquisition bottleneck that limits automation.

entries.title: The Expert Validation Framework: Enabling Domain Expert Control in AI Engineering
entries.author: AI Systems Research Team
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Who controls AI?
entries.hook: Domain experts should not have to learn code to validate AI systems.
entries.why_it_matters: Bridges Instruments 1 and 5: Ensures domain expertise and ethical reasoning can be embedded directly into AI systems without requiring technical translation.
entries.summary: Proposes a validation framework that enables domain experts to directly control and verify AI system behaviors, preserving expert judgment in algorithmic decision-making. Additional context needed.

entries.title: Polanyi's Paradox and the Shape of Employment Growth
entries.author: Autor, David
questions: Will AI take my job?
How fast is this happening?
entries.hook: Michael Polanyi said 'We can know more than we can tell.
entries.why_it_matters: Foundational to all five instruments: Polanyi's Paradox explains why tacit knowledge, judgment, and human intuition remain irreducible in professional work.
entries.summary: Economist David Autor formalized Polanyi's Paradox in employment context, showing how tasks that are hard to codify resist automation and remain human-centric. Additional context needed.

entries.title: Polanyi's Revenge and AI's New Romance with Tacit Knowledge
entries.author: Communications of the ACM Editorial Team
questions: Can AI think or feel?
How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: Machine learning broke the code-writing requirement for automation.
entries.why_it_matters: Instruments 1 and 2: Shows how modern AI doesn't overcome Polanyi's Paradox—it reinforces why human verification and domain expertise remain critical.
entries.summary: Argues that recent ML advances don't solve the Polanyi Paradox but instead make the problem more complex, requiring deeper human expertise in verification and oversight. Additional context needed.

entries.title: Critical Thinking in the Age of Generative AI
entries.author: Academy of Management Learning & Education Research Team
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Will AI take my job?
entries.hook: Students who use AI for thinking show weaker reasoning skills than those who struggle.
entries.why_it_matters: Instrument 2 (Critical Thinking): Demonstrates empirically why humans must maintain cognitive rigor even—especially—when working with AI.
entries.summary: Research showing that overreliance on AI for complex reasoning tasks erodes critical thinking skills, arguing for deliberate cognitive engagement in AI-era work. Additional context needed.

entries.title: AI Won't Make the Call: Why Human Judgment Still Drives Innovation
entries.author: Harvard Business School BIGS
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
Will AI take my job?
entries.hook: AI generates infinite options.
entries.why_it_matters: Instruments 2 and 3: Shows why the human ability to distinguish signal from noise—to judge what matters—cannot be delegated to machines.
entries.summary: Argues that while AI can enhance productivity, human judgment about what is worth creating remains the irreplaceable differentiator in innovation. Additional context needed.

entries.title: Deconstructing Taste: Toward a Human-Centered AI Framework for Modeling Consumer Aesthetic Perceptions
entries.author: AI Aesthetics Research Team
